dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 170 responding to a reported seizure at the --Address Redacted-- in [CITY]. EMS is to confirm alertness and administer emergency care if needed.
Hi, I'm Ryan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What led you to call 911 today?
My roommate had two seizures in the last hour. I found him laying on the floor and called 911.
Patient, can you tell me your name? Can you open your eyes for me?
Can you follow my finger with your eyes?
Tell me the name of the city we're in. Are you able to make any sounds or words?
Can you squeeze my finger with your left hand? How about your right?
Patient is breathing independently.
Respirations are rapid-counting about 30 breaths per minute.
Pulse is 101. Strong and regular.
Breathing is 30 per minute. Still rapid, not increased work of breathing or signs of distress.
Blood pressure is 150 over 83. Within normal range.
Skin is warm, pink, and dry. Peripheral pulses are strong and easily palpable.
Mr. Smith, on a scale from 0 to 10, how much pain or discomfort are you feeling right now?
He's not indicating any pain. He was unconscious during the seizures.
Airway appears clear with no obstruction.
Mr. Smith, are you having trouble understanding what I'm saying or following instructions?
Partner, check for any visible medications or pill bottles in the area.
Mr. Smith, could you tell me what medications you're taking right now?
He takes lisinopril, metformin, and risperidone. I'll show you the bottle.
Can we check the bottles for dosage and strength printed on the pills for documentation?
Yes. I'll check the bottles for you.
Skin appears normal. No rashes, wounds, abrasions, or bite marks noted. Let's continue the head-to-toe check.
Pupils are equal and reactive to light. No sign of head trauma or bleeding.
Neck is smooth with no masses or stiffness found.
Glucose is 230. Elevated, no signs of acute concern.
Oxygen saturation is at 98%.
4-lead EKG monitoring initiated. Rhythm is currently in sinus.
Partner, let's secure the cervical collar to prevent any movement injury during transport.
Cervical collar in place. Gently moving patient to sitting position for airway stability.
Pulse is now at 96. Still strong and consistent.
Breathing is up to 31 per minute. Need to keep an eye on.
Blood pressure is now 141 over 83. Mild drop but not concerning.
Vital signs, seizure frequency, and medical history align with protocol 3-12 for non-traumatic seizures.
IV access is placed in left forearm. Keeping it secure as directed.
Protocol 3-12 is confirmed for seizure management. All steps to transport are complete.
What hospital do you usually go to for your care?
His usual is [COMPANY_NAME]. Either is fine with us.
Moving patient to the stretcher and securing with a 5-point harness on the Reeves board.
Patient is on the board and ready for transport. Safe to move now.
Latest pulse reading is 98. Regular and unchanged.
Breathing has increased to 39 per minute. Need to continue close observation.
Blood pressure is 133 over 83. Still stable without acute concerns.
Mr. Smith, when was the last time you had food or drink prior to the first seizure?
He had some toast with coffee earlier. No alcohol observed.
What happened right before the first episode began?
He reported a flash of light and dizziness. First seizure was about 10 seconds, the second followed after 10 minutes.
Glucose remains at 230 on recheck.
O2 remains at 96%.
EKG reads sinus rhythm again. No arrhythmia noted.
How was his level of awareness after the seizures?
He remained unoriented for over an hour and only came back to full awareness after that.
Heart rate is 98; respiratory rate is 39; BP 133/83; O2 is still at 96.
Launching ambulance to [COMPANY_NAME] now. Keep patient steady during the move.
We're en route. Hospital destination confirmed. Patient remains stable.
Glucose remains at 230 after another check.
O2 sat remains at 96% without any drop.
Sinus rhythm is consistent. No developing issues found.
ED nurse will take over once we arrive. Prepare for safe handoff now.
ED nurse is now taking care of the patient.
All interventions and monitoring in progress have been documented.
All patient information and medications have been transferred to the hospital team.
